questions.prompt: What event in October 1993 marked a decisive turning point in Russia's post-Soviet political development?

choices.text: Yeltsin ordered tanks to shell the Russian parliament building
The Communist Party was officially banned in Russia
choices.note: The Communist Party was briefly suspended after the 1991 coup but was not permanently banned; it returned as a political party.

choices.text: Russia held its first free and fair presidential election
choices.note: Russia's first competitive presidential election was in 1991, not 1993.

choices.text: The Russian Supreme Soviet voted to dissolve itself peacefully
choices.note: The parliament refused to dissolve, leading to an armed standoff that Yeltsin resolved by force.

questions.explanation: In October 1993, a constitutional crisis between President Yeltsin and the Russian parliament escalated into an armed confrontation. Yeltsin ordered the military to shell the White House (parliament building), defeating his opponents. This cleared the way for a new constitution with strong presidential powers, which critics argue planted the seeds for later authoritarianism.

questions.prompt: Ukraine's 2004 Orange Revolution was triggered primarily by what event?

choices.text: Widespread fraud in the presidential election favoring Viktor Yanukovych
The assassination of a prominent opposition journalist
choices.note: While journalist Georgiy Gongadze was murdered in 2000, this occurred years before the Orange Revolution.

choices.text: Russia's annexation of Crimea
choices.note: Russia's annexation of Crimea occurred a decade later, in 2014.

choices.text: An economic crisis caused by the collapse of the hryvnia
choices.note: There was no major currency crisis in 2004; the protests were driven by electoral fraud.

questions.explanation: The Orange Revolution erupted after the disputed November 2004 presidential runoff, in which massive electoral fraud favored the Kremlin-backed Viktor Yanukovych over Viktor Yushchenko. Weeks of peaceful protests in Kyiv's Maidan (Independence Square) led to a Supreme Court ruling annulling the results and ordering a re-vote, which Yushchenko won.

questions.prompt: What constitutional change did Putin's 2020 amendments most notably introduce?

choices.text: They reset Putin's presidential term count, allowing him to remain in power until 2036
They abolished the office of Prime Minister
choices.note: The Prime Minister position was retained; Mikhail Mishustin held the role.

choices.text: They established Russia as a parliamentary system
choices.note: Russia remained a presidential system with enhanced, not reduced, presidential powers.

choices.text: They granted independence to Chechnya
choices.note: The amendments actually prohibited the alienation of Russian territory.

questions.explanation: The 2020 constitutional amendments included a provision 'resetting' Putin's previous terms, allowing him to serve two additional six-year terms (potentially through 2036). The amendments also enshrined conservative social values, strengthened presidential power over courts, and prohibited the alienation of Russian territory.

questions.prompt: The 2013–2014 Euromaidan protests in Ukraine began in response to what decision by President Yanukovych?

choices.text: His refusal to sign the EU Association Agreement
His decision to join the Eurasian Economic Union
choices.note: Ukraine did not join the Eurasian Economic Union; Yanukovych suspended the EU agreement under Russian pressure, but the Eurasian Union was not the immediate trigger.

choices.text: His attempt to amend the constitution to extend his term
choices.note: Yanukovych's term was not at issue; the protests were about Ukraine's geopolitical orientation.

choices.text: His arrest of opposition leader Yulia Tymoshenko
choices.note: Tymoshenko was imprisoned in 2011 in a politically motivated case, but the Euromaidan protests in late 2013 were triggered by the EU decision.

questions.explanation: The Euromaidan protests began on November 21, 2013, after Yanukovych suspended preparations for signing the EU Association Agreement under heavy pressure from Russia. What started as a pro-EU rally grew into a broader movement against corruption and authoritarian governance, culminating in Yanukovych's ouster in February 2014.

questions.prompt: Political scientist M. Steven Fish argued that Russia's democratic failure in the 1990s was significantly caused by:

choices.text: The adoption of a superpresidential constitution that concentrated too much power in the executive
The absence of any democratic traditions in Russian history
choices.note: Fish did not argue that cultural determinism explains democratic failure; his focus was on institutional design.

choices.text: Western interference in Russian domestic politics
choices.note: Fish's argument centered on domestic institutional choices, not foreign interference.

choices.text: The rapid pace of economic liberalization
choices.note: Fish actually argued that insufficient economic reform, not too-rapid reform, was part of the problem.

questions.explanation: In his influential work, M. Steven Fish argued that the 1993 Russian constitution created a 'superpresidential' system that concentrated excessive power in the presidency at the expense of the legislature, parties, and civil society. This institutional imbalance, he contended, was a key factor undermining democratic development in Russia.

questions.prompt: Who did Boris Yeltsin defeat in the second round of Russia's 1996 presidential election?

choices.text: Gennady Zyuganov
Vladimir Zhirinovsky
choices.note: Zhirinovsky was a nationalist candidate in Russian elections, but he was not Yeltsin's runoff opponent in 1996.

choices.text: Grigory Yavlinsky
choices.note: Yavlinsky ran in the first round, but he did not advance to the second round.

choices.text: Yevgeny Primakov
choices.note: Primakov later became prime minister, but he was not the Communist candidate in the 1996 presidential runoff.

questions.explanation: Yeltsin's 1996 reelection against Communist Party leader Gennady Zyuganov was a pivotal moment in post-Soviet Russia. Despite Yeltsin's low approval ratings, the Kremlin, allied oligarchs, and much of the major media establishment mobilized heavily to prevent a Communist return to power.

questions.prompt: In comparative politics, a 'competitive authoritarian' regime is one in which:

choices.text: Formal democratic institutions exist, but incumbents skew the media, courts, and elections so heavily that competition is unfair
The military rules openly without elections or opposition parties
choices.note: That describes a more straightforward military dictatorship, not a competitive authoritarian system.

choices.text: All major parties compete on a level playing field and power alternates regularly
choices.note: That is closer to a liberal democracy than to competitive authoritarianism.

choices.text: Political power is shared equally between an elected president and a hereditary monarch
choices.note: Competitive authoritarianism is about unfair but still meaningful electoral competition, not monarchy.

questions.explanation: Steven Levitsky and Lucan Way used the term 'competitive authoritarianism' for regimes that retain elections and other democratic institutions but manipulate them so systematically that incumbents enjoy overwhelming advantages. Russia under Putin has often been analyzed through this lens, especially as independent media and opposition politics narrowed.

questions.prompt: What institutional change emerged from Ukraine's 2004 constitutional compromise during the Orange Revolution?

choices.text: Some powers shifted from the presidency to parliament and the prime minister
The presidency was abolished entirely
choices.note: Ukraine kept a president; the compromise reduced presidential dominance rather than eliminating the office.

choices.text: Ukraine was turned into a federal state with autonomous regions
choices.note: The 2004 compromise was about the balance of powers at the center, not the federalization of Ukraine.

choices.text: Direct parliamentary elections were cancelled
choices.note: The compromise did not cancel parliamentary elections; it altered the institutional balance within Ukraine's semi-presidential system.

questions.explanation: As part of the political settlement to end the Orange Revolution crisis, Ukraine adopted constitutional changes that reduced presidential power and strengthened parliament and the prime minister. Those reforms, implemented in 2006, reshaped Ukraine's semi-presidential system and contributed to a more contested, pluralistic political arena.

questions.prompt: What did observers mean by 'managed democracy' in Putin's Russia?

choices.text: Elections and parties were retained, but politics was tightly controlled from above to produce limited competition
Russia had shifted to direct democracy through constant nationwide referendums
choices.note: The term did not mean mass direct democracy; it referred to a top-down system in which electoral politics remained but was carefully stage-managed.

choices.text: Independent regional governors had gained enough autonomy to check the Kremlin
choices.note: Under Putin, the Kremlin worked to reduce regional autonomy rather than strengthen it.

choices.text: The military had replaced civilian institutions and ruled openly
choices.note: Russia kept formal civilian institutions and elections, which is why the term emphasized management rather than outright military rule.

questions.explanation: Managed democracy described a system in which elections, parties, and formal constitutional structures remained in place, but the Kremlin sharply constrained who could compete and what information voters received. Media control, party engineering, and administrative pressure helped create the appearance of democracy while protecting the ruling center from real uncertainty.

questions.prompt: Compared with Russia in the 2000s, why did Ukraine remain more politically pluralistic?

choices.text: Competing regional elites and oligarchic groups made it harder for any single center to monopolize power
Ukraine lacked oligarchs and large business interests in politics
choices.note: Ukraine had powerful oligarchs; their competition with one another was part of what sustained political pluralism.

choices.text: Ukrainian presidents had stronger formal powers than Russian presidents
choices.note: If anything, Russia's constitution was more strongly superpresidential than Ukraine's.

choices.text: Russia used proportional representation while Ukraine did not
choices.note: Electoral rules mattered, but they were not the main structural reason Ukraine remained more pluralistic than Russia.

questions.explanation: Ukraine's politics were often messy and heavily influenced by oligarchs, but power was dispersed across regions, business groups, and institutions in ways that made full recentralization difficult. Russia, by contrast, saw the Kremlin consolidate authority more successfully in the 2000s through media control, regional recentralization, and tighter management of party competition.
sources: Popova and Shevel, Russia and Ukraine: Entangled Histories, Diverging States

questions.prompt: What made Ukraine's 1994 presidential election especially significant in the post-Soviet region?

choices.text: It produced a peaceful transfer of presidential power through a competitive election
It abolished the presidency and replaced it with parliamentary rule
choices.note: Ukraine kept the presidency; the 1994 contest was about who would hold the office, not whether the office would exist.

choices.text: It was the first election in which the Communist Party was legally allowed to compete
choices.note: The recreated Communist Party was active in Ukrainian politics in the 1990s, but the special significance of 1994 was the peaceful transfer of power.

choices.text: It brought Rukh to power as the dominant governing party
choices.note: Rukh was influential but fragmented and never became Ukraine's dominant governing party.

questions.explanation: Leonid Kravchuk's defeat by Leonid Kuchma in 1994 is often treated as a landmark because an incumbent president left office after a competitive election rather than through force. In a region where many former Soviet leaders clung to power, Ukraine demonstrated that peaceful turnover was possible even amid severe economic crisis and regional polarization.

questions.prompt: In contrast to Russia, what issue most strongly structured Ukraine's early post-Soviet political competition in the 1990s?

choices.text: Questions of nationality, language, and relations with Russia
Debates over how quickly to privatize heavy industry
choices.note: Economic reform mattered, but the lectures emphasize that nationality issues were more central in Ukraine's early post-Soviet politics than market reform debates.

choices.text: Disputes over whether to continue the Chechen war
choices.note: The Chechen wars were a Russian issue, not the main cleavage structuring Ukrainian politics.

choices.text: Arguments about restoring the Soviet Union under Communist rule
choices.note: Pro-Soviet and pro-Russian views existed, but the key axis in Ukraine was broader nationality policy rather than a simple restorationist program.

questions.explanation: Where Russian politics in the 1990s was heavily polarized around economic reform, Ukrainian politics was more shaped by identity questions: language policy, the status of Ukrainian culture, and how closely the country should align with Russia. That helps explain why regional voting patterns in Ukraine often looked different from Russia's class- and reform-centered conflicts.

questions.prompt: What best describes Rukh (the People's Movement of Ukraine) in the early post-Soviet period?

choices.text: A national-democratic umbrella movement that helped mobilize support for Ukrainian statehood but later splintered
A Kremlin-backed electoral machine centered in Donetsk
choices.note: That description fits later eastern networks such as the Party of Regions, not Rukh.

choices.text: A successor organization to the Soviet KGB in Ukraine
choices.note: Rukh was a political movement, not a security institution.

choices.text: A monarchist party seeking restoration of the Romanov dynasty
choices.note: Rukh was associated with national-democratic politics, not monarchism.

questions.explanation: Rukh emerged under glasnost as a broad national-democratic movement rather than a tightly disciplined party. It mattered politically because it linked democratization with Ukrainian national revival, but internal divisions and a fragmented party system kept it from becoming a stable dominant force in the 1990s.

questions.prompt: Which statement about Ukraine's 1996 constitution is most accurate?

choices.text: It strengthened the presidency but was adopted through a negotiated process within existing constitutional rules, not by an extra-legal referendum
It eliminated the presidency and made the prime minister the only national executive
choices.note: The 1996 constitution did not abolish the presidency; it strengthened it relative to the pre-1996 arrangement.

choices.text: It turned Ukraine into a federal state with constitutionally sovereign regions
choices.note: Ukraine remained a unitary state, even though Crimea retained a special autonomous status.

choices.text: It restored the 2004 Orange Revolution reforms that weakened the presidency
choices.note: The 2004 reforms came later; the 1996 constitution preceded them and initially strengthened presidential power.

questions.explanation: Kuchma pushed for a stronger presidency, but Ukraine's 1996 constitution was still more of a compromise than Russia's 1993 settlement. Because it was passed through existing procedures rather than imposed after a violent showdown, it did not carry the same origin story as Russia's superpresidential constitution.

questions.prompt: Why does Lucan Way describe Leonid Kravchuk as a 'democratizer by default' in early independent Ukraine?

choices.text: Because neither Kravchuk nor his rivals were strong enough to monopolize power, allowing relatively competitive politics to persist
Because Kravchuk immediately built a strong liberal party that institutionalized democracy
choices.note: Way's point is almost the opposite: Kravchuk lacked a strong, well-organized ruling machine.

choices.text: Because Ukraine joined the European Union during his presidency
choices.note: Ukraine did not join the EU in the 1990s, and Way does not explain early pluralism that way.

choices.text: Because he rewrote the constitution to prevent any future president from exercising executive power
choices.note: Kravchuk did not neutralize the presidency in that way.

questions.explanation: Way's argument is that pluralism in Ukraine often survived less because elites were committed democrats than because state institutions and ruling parties were too weak to shut opponents out. Kravchuk's inability to control the administrative apparatus or coercive institutions made a peaceful electoral defeat possible in 1994.

questions.prompt: What was distinctive about Leonid Kuchma's 'multi-party ruling party' in late-1990s Ukraine?

choices.text: He relied on several competing oligarch-backed parties and patronage networks rather than a single centralized ruling party
He outlawed all parties except the Communist Party
choices.note: Ukraine's system under Kuchma was manipulative and oligarchic, but it was not a one-party Communist restoration.

choices.text: He built a disciplined mass ideological party modeled on Soviet-era Leninism
choices.note: Kuchma's support base was not built around a coherent mass ideology; it depended on patronage and elite bargaining.

choices.text: He refused all cooperation with business elites and based his rule solely on trade unions
choices.note: Oligarchic and regional business interests were central to Kuchma's governing system.

questions.explanation: Kuchma strengthened the state more than Kravchuk had, but he still did not create a fully institutionalized ruling party on the Russian model that emerged later under Putin. Instead, he balanced multiple elite groups whose media, money, and regional machines could support him but could also defect.

questions.prompt: How did Boris Yeltsin try to keep the Russian Federation together in the mid-1990s as regional leaders pressed for autonomy?

choices.text: By signing bilateral power-sharing agreements that granted some regions special autonomy and resources
By abolishing all regional governments and ruling entirely through military districts
choices.note: That would resemble a far more centralized system than Russia had under Yeltsin in the 1990s.

choices.text: By converting Russia into a unitary state and eliminating republics from the constitution
choices.note: Russia remained a federation, and Yeltsin relied on asymmetrical deals rather than eliminating federal subjects.

choices.text: By allowing every region to hold binding independence referendums
choices.note: Yeltsin bargained over autonomy, but he did not create a general right of secession.

questions.explanation: Yeltsin's strategy is often called asymmetrical federalism. Deals with places such as Tatarstan gave regions greater control over taxes, resources, and other prerogatives, which helped avoid immediate disintegration but also weakened the center and set up later recentralization under Putin.

questions.prompt: What did the 'Kuchmagate' scandal center on in Ukraine around 2000?

choices.text: Secret recordings that appeared to implicate President Kuchma in the abduction of journalist Georgiy Gongadze
Evidence that Kuchma had ordered tanks to fire on parliament
choices.note: That refers to Russia's 1993 crisis under Yeltsin, not Ukraine under Kuchma.

choices.text: Proof that Ukraine had secretly joined NATO
choices.note: The scandal was about domestic abuse of power and a murdered journalist, not NATO membership.

choices.text: A Supreme Court ruling that annulled a presidential election
choices.note: The Supreme Court annulled the fraudulent 2004 runoff, but that was a later Orange Revolution event.

questions.explanation: Kuchmagate badly damaged Kuchma by linking his presidency to coercion against the press and corruption. Although the anti-Kuchma protests did not topple him, they exposed the limits of his legitimacy and helped set the stage for the more successful mobilization of 2004.

questions.prompt: In post-Soviet Russian political language, what is the usual distinction between rossiiskii and russkii?

choices.text: Rossiiskii refers to the civic identity of the Russian state, while russkii refers more specifically to ethnic Russian identity
Rossiiskii refers to Soviet identity, while russkii refers to post-Soviet citizenship
choices.note: The distinction is not Soviet versus post-Soviet; it is primarily civic-state versus ethnic identity.

choices.text: Rossiiskii refers only to residents of Moscow, while russkii refers to residents of the provinces
choices.note: These are not regional labels within Russia.

choices.text: Rossiiskii refers to Orthodox believers, while russkii refers to secular citizens
choices.note: The terms are not mainly about religion.

questions.explanation: The distinction matters because post-Soviet Russian leaders often preferred a civic definition of the polity in a multiethnic federation rather than an ethnic one.

questions.prompt: Why did Leonid Kuchma ultimately back Viktor Yanukovych as his preferred successor in Ukraine's 2004 presidential election?

choices.text: Yanukovych had strong organizational networks in Donetsk and a machine that could mobilize support
Yanukovych was the most popular politician in western Ukraine
choices.note: Yanukovych's strength was concentrated in the east and south, not in western Ukraine.

choices.text: Yanukovych had led the Orange Revolution against fraud
choices.note: Yanukovych was the regime-backed candidate opposed by the Orange Revolution.

choices.text: Yanukovych was the only major politician with no ties to oligarchic interests
choices.note: His rise was closely connected to Donetsk-based elite and business networks.

questions.explanation: Way argues that Yanukovych's appeal to Kuchma lay less in charisma than in organization. As a Donetsk power broker and former governor, he brought a regional machine and patronage networks that seemed better suited to preserving the regime than a weaker or more purely personal successor would have been.

questions.prompt: Which institution formally resolved Ukraine's 2004 presidential election crisis by ordering a repeat runoff?

choices.text: The Supreme Court of Ukraine
The Constitutional Court of Russia
choices.note: Russia's courts did not decide Ukraine's 2004 election dispute.

choices.text: The Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe
choices.note: International observers criticized the fraud, but the legal order for a rerun came from Ukraine's own Supreme Court.

choices.text: The Ukrainian military high command
choices.note: The military did not settle the dispute by decree; a court ruling did.

questions.explanation: On December 3, 2004, the Supreme Court ruled that fraud in the runoff had been too extensive to determine a legitimate winner and ordered a new vote. That legal resolution helped prevent a more violent outcome and gave the Orange Revolution an institutional path to victory.

questions.prompt: What most weakened the Orange coalition after Yushchenko's 2004 victory?

choices.text: Persistent rivalry between Yushchenko and Tymoshenko, combined with the coalition's institutional fragmentation
A legal ban on all opposition parties except the Party of Regions
choices.note: The Party of Regions remained powerful, but Ukraine did not legally ban all other opposition parties after 2004.

choices.text: Immediate military occupation of Kyiv by Russia
choices.note: That did not happen in the Orange period.

choices.text: The abolition of parliamentary elections in favor of presidential appointments
choices.note: Ukraine continued to hold parliamentary elections; in fact, coalition politics in parliament helped fuel instability.

questions.explanation: The Orange camp was united against fraud, not by a coherent governing program or stable party structure. Once in office, conflicts between Yushchenko and Tymoshenko, plus competition among oligarchic and party blocs, made sustained reform and coalition discipline difficult.

questions.prompt: What made the Party of Regions under Viktor Yanukovych different from the governing arrangements used by Kuchma?

choices.text: It functioned as Ukraine's first true party in power, concentrating control over both executive and legislature more effectively than Kuchma's looser coalition system
It was a small western Ukrainian protest party with little access to the state
choices.note: The Party of Regions was rooted in eastern networks and became the core of state power under Yanukovych.

choices.text: It rejected regional patronage and relied only on volunteer activists
choices.note: Regional patronage networks were central to the party's strength.

choices.text: It was created mainly to represent Crimean Tatars in parliament
choices.note: The Party of Regions was not a minority-representation party of that kind.

questions.explanation: Way describes Yanukovych's machine as more centralized and disciplined than Kuchma's earlier 'multi-party ruling party.' By forcing allies into a single dominant structure, Yanukovych came closer than any earlier Ukrainian leader to building a real ruling party on the model of a consolidated party of power.

questions.prompt: What institutional move most strengthened Viktor Yanukovych after he became president in 2010?

choices.text: The reversal of the 2004 constitutional reforms, which shifted power back toward the presidency
The abolition of parliament and replacement of the Rada with a military council
choices.note: Yanukovych concentrated power, but he did not formally abolish parliament.

choices.text: The creation of a new federal constitution granting sovereignty to the oblasts
choices.note: Ukraine remained a unitary state under Yanukovych.

choices.text: The immediate legalization of dual citizenship with Russia nationwide
choices.note: That was not the central institutional move that strengthened his presidency in 2010.

questions.explanation: By undoing the Orange-era constitutional balance, Yanukovych regained powers that had previously been shifted toward parliament and the prime minister. Combined with control over the courts and a disciplined Party of Regions machine, this helped him bring Ukraine closer to competitive authoritarian rule.

questions.prompt: What did Ukraine's 2012 Kolesnychenko-Kivalov language law allow?

choices.text: It allowed a minority language to be used in official settings in regions where that minority made up at least 10 percent of the population
It made Russian a co-equal second state language across all of Ukraine
choices.note: The law expanded regional official use of minority languages, but it did not make Russian a second state language nationwide.

choices.text: It let regional councils adopt a minority language as the sole language of administration even without meeting a population threshold
choices.note: The law used a numerical threshold and did not authorize regions to displace Ukrainian entirely from administration.

choices.text: It granted official regional language status only where minority speakers formed a majority of the population
choices.note: The threshold was much lower than a majority: 10 percent of the local population.

questions.explanation: The law was controversial because it gave Russian and other minority languages greater official standing in places where they were widely spoken. Its passage triggered fierce protests and later became politically important again after Maidan, when debate over repeal fed tensions that Russia exploited.

questions.prompt: Which statement best reflects Ukraine's post-1991 nation-building strategy?

choices.text: It was broadly civic and inclusive: residents of Ukraine became citizens regardless of ethnicity, and the state did not reserve citizenship for ethnic Ukrainians alone
It defined citizenship exclusively by ethnic Ukrainian ancestry
choices.note: Ukraine did not construct citizenship on a purely ethnic basis after independence.

choices.text: It preserved the Soviet practice of listing nationality in all internal passports as a core state principle
choices.note: Post-Soviet Ukrainian passports did not retain nationality in that way.

choices.text: It prohibited Russian speakers from holding public office
choices.note: Language politics were contentious, but Ukraine did not legally bar Russian speakers from office.

questions.explanation: Course materials stress that Ukrainian nation-building was generally civic rather than explicitly ethnic. That did not eliminate language and regional conflict, but it helps explain why many Russian-speaking or ethnically mixed citizens could still be incorporated into a Ukrainian political nation.

questions.prompt: What was the main purpose of Putin's creation of federal districts in 2000?

choices.text: To place regional authorities under tighter Kremlin oversight and bring regional laws into line with federal law
To prepare Russia for accession to the European Union
choices.note: The federal districts were a domestic recentralization measure, not an EU-accession reform.

choices.text: To replace the armed forces with civilian governors
choices.note: The reform concerned administrative oversight of the regions, not abolition of the military.

choices.text: To give each region the right to negotiate its own foreign policy
choices.note: Putin's reform reduced, rather than expanded, regional autonomy.

questions.explanation: The new federal districts, headed by presidential envoys, were an early pillar of Putin's 'vertical of power.' They helped the Kremlin supervise governors, discipline regional elites, and roll back the looser asymmetrical federalism of the Yeltsin years.

questions.prompt: After the 2004 Beslan school siege, what major change did Putin make to Russia's regional political system?

choices.text: He ended direct gubernatorial elections and moved to presidential appointment of governors
He restored direct elections for the Federation Council
choices.note: The post-Beslan change went in the direction of tighter executive control, not broader regional electoral autonomy.

choices.text: He allowed republics to choose whether to remain inside the Russian Federation
choices.note: Putin did not create a right of regional secession after Beslan.

choices.text: He transferred control over the security services to regional legislatures
choices.note: The reform strengthened the center rather than devolving coercive power to the regions.

questions.explanation: Putin justified the change as a security response to terrorism, but politically it was a major step in recentralization. By reducing regional electoral autonomy, the Kremlin further weakened governors as independent political actors and tightened the presidential chain of command.

questions.prompt: In 2000s Russia, what was United Russia's primary political role?

choices.text: To serve as a 'party of power' that organized legislative majorities for the Kremlin and limited genuine parliamentary competition
To function as the main anti-Kremlin opposition party in the Duma
choices.note: United Russia was aligned with the Kremlin, not its main parliamentary opponent.

choices.text: To promote regional sovereignty against Moscow
choices.note: United Russia was central to Moscow's recentralization, not to regional resistance.

choices.text: To replace presidential elections with selection by the Communist Party
choices.note: United Russia was not a restoration of Communist rule; it was a pro-Kremlin electoral machine.

questions.explanation: United Russia mattered less as an ideological party than as an institutional tool of regime management. Its dominance in the Duma and regional legislatures helped turn legislatures into bodies that largely ratified executive decisions rather than independently checking presidential power.

questions.prompt: What was the name of Mikhail Gorbachev's policy of political openness, introduced in the mid-1980s?

choices.text: Glasnost
Perestroika
choices.note: Perestroika referred to economic restructuring, not political openness.

choices.text: Demokratizatsiya
choices.note: Demokratizatsiya (democratization) was a related concept but not the specific policy of openness.

choices.text: Uskoreniye
choices.note: Uskoreniye (acceleration) was Gorbachev's initial economic program focused on industrial output.

questions.explanation: Glasnost (meaning 'openness') was Gorbachev's policy of increasing transparency in government and expanding press freedom. It complemented perestroika (economic restructuring) and together they unintentionally accelerated the unraveling of the Soviet system.

questions.prompt: What was the outcome of the August 1991 coup attempt against Gorbachev?

choices.text: It failed within three days, accelerating the Soviet Union's dissolution
It succeeded and installed a new hardline Soviet government
choices.note: The coup collapsed due to popular resistance and military refusal to use force.

choices.text: It led to a prolonged civil war between reformers and hardliners
choices.note: There was no civil war; the coup's rapid failure discredited hardliners and empowered independence movements.

choices.text: Gorbachev negotiated a compromise that preserved the Soviet Union for another five years
choices.note: Though Gorbachev returned to power, the coup fatally weakened central authority. The USSR dissolved by December 1991.

questions.explanation: The August 1991 coup by Communist hardliners aimed to reverse Gorbachev's reforms but collapsed in three days, largely due to Boris Yeltsin's public resistance in Moscow. The failed coup fatally undermined the Communist Party's legitimacy and accelerated the declarations of independence by Soviet republics.

questions.prompt: In Ukraine's December 1, 1991 independence referendum, approximately what percentage of voters supported independence?

choices.text: Over 90%
About 70%
choices.note: The actual figure was over 92%, reflecting overwhelming support even in regions with large Russian-speaking populations.

choices.text: A narrow majority of about 52%
choices.note: Support was far higher than a narrow majority — over 92% voted in favor.

choices.text: About 80%
choices.note: While close, the actual figure exceeded 92%, with majorities in every single region of Ukraine.

questions.explanation: Over 92% of voters supported Ukrainian independence in the December 1, 1991 referendum, with turnout above 84%. Notably, every region of Ukraine voted in favor, including Crimea (54%) and eastern regions with large Russian-speaking populations. This result was pivotal in convincing Yeltsin that the Soviet Union could not continue without Ukraine.

questions.prompt: Which of the following was NOT a major factor contributing to the collapse of the Soviet Union?

choices.text: A foreign military invasion that destabilized the central government
Nationalist movements in the Soviet republics demanding independence
choices.note: Nationalist movements were a key driver, particularly in the Baltics, Ukraine, and the Caucasus.

choices.text: Economic stagnation and the failure of central planning
choices.note: The Soviet economy had been stagnating since the 1970s, undermining the system's legitimacy.

choices.text: Gorbachev's reforms unintentionally weakening Communist Party control
choices.note: Glasnost and perestroika opened space for dissent and weakened the party's monopoly on power.

questions.explanation: The Soviet Union collapsed due to a combination of internal factors: economic stagnation, nationalist movements, the failure of reforms, and the loss of Communist Party legitimacy. No foreign military invasion played a role — the collapse was driven by domestic dynamics, though the Cold War's economic pressures contributed to the broader context.

questions.prompt: What did the repeal of Article 6 of the Soviet constitution in 1990 do?

choices.text: It ended the Communist Party's constitutional monopoly on political power
It created direct popular elections for the Soviet presidency
choices.note: The Soviet presidency was created in 1990, but the office was not established by repealing Article 6 and was not filled by a direct popular vote.

choices.text: It granted the Soviet republics an automatic legal right to secede immediately
choices.note: The Soviet constitution had long contained a formal right of secession on paper; Article 6 concerned the Communist Party's leading role.

choices.text: It launched rapid price liberalization across the Soviet economy
choices.note: Price reform was part of broader economic debates under perestroika, not the purpose of repealing Article 6.

questions.explanation: Article 6 had enshrined the Communist Party of the Soviet Union as the 'leading and guiding force' of Soviet society. Its repeal in 1990 opened the way for genuine multiparty politics, weakened party control over the state, and accelerated the political fragmentation of the late Soviet system.

questions.prompt: What was the purpose of the March 1991 all-Union referendum held in most Soviet republics?

choices.text: To ask voters whether they wanted to preserve the USSR as a renewed federation
To determine whether Russia should immediately adopt capitalism
choices.note: The referendum was about the future of the Soviet Union, not a straight vote on capitalism in Russia.

choices.text: To ratify the Belavezha Accords dissolving the USSR
choices.note: The Belavezha Accords were signed in December 1991, months after the March referendum.

choices.text: To vote on abolishing the Communist Party outright
choices.note: The referendum did not ask voters to ban the Communist Party; it focused on preserving a reformed union.

questions.explanation: The March 1991 referendum asked whether citizens supported preserving the Soviet Union as a 'renewed federation of equal sovereign republics.' Majorities in the participating republics voted yes, but several republics, including the Baltic states, boycotted it. The result showed that reforming the USSR still had support, but only in a shrinking and increasingly divided political space.

questions.prompt: The New Union Treaty that hardliners sought to block in August 1991 would have:

choices.text: Transformed the USSR into a looser federation that gave more power to the republics
Restored a Stalin-style command system and centralized police rule
choices.note: The treaty aimed to decentralize the union, not revive a rigidly centralized Stalinist system.

choices.text: Abolished republican institutions and returned all authority to Moscow ministries
choices.note: The entire controversy was that the treaty shifted authority away from Moscow and toward the republics.

choices.text: Readmitted Eastern European satellite states into a rebuilt Soviet bloc
choices.note: The treaty dealt with the internal structure of the USSR, not the reconstitution of Soviet control over Eastern Europe.

questions.explanation: Gorbachev's proposed New Union Treaty was intended to preserve the Soviet Union in a much looser form by granting the republics greater autonomy while keeping a shared federal framework. Hardliners viewed it as the effective dismantling of the USSR and launched the August 1991 coup on the eve of its planned signing.

questions.prompt: Who became the first popularly elected president of the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic in June 1991?

choices.text: Boris Yeltsin
Mikhail Gorbachev
choices.note: Gorbachev was president of the Soviet Union, not the Russian republic, and he was elected by the Congress of People's Deputies rather than by a direct popular vote.

choices.text: Alexander Rutskoi
choices.note: Rutskoi was elected vice president on Yeltsin's ticket, not president.

choices.text: Nikolai Ryzhkov
choices.note: Ryzhkov ran against Yeltsin in 1991 but lost the presidential election.

questions.explanation: Yeltsin's June 1991 victory gave him an independent electoral mandate separate from Gorbachev and the Soviet center. That legitimacy became crucial during the August coup, when Yeltsin emerged as the most visible symbol of resistance to the hardliners.

questions.prompt: What did the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic's June 1990 Declaration of State Sovereignty assert?

choices.text: That Russian laws would take precedence over Soviet union laws on Russian territory
That Russia was immediately withdrawing from the Soviet Union
choices.note: The declaration asserted sovereignty within the USSR, but it was not an immediate declaration of Russian independence.

choices.text: That Gorbachev would simultaneously serve as Russia's president
choices.note: The declaration did not make Gorbachev head of the Russian republic.

choices.text: That private land ownership was fully legalized across the USSR
choices.note: The declaration was about sovereignty and legal authority, not a blanket privatization of land throughout the Soviet Union.

questions.explanation: Russia's sovereignty declaration was a major step in the late Soviet 'war of laws,' in which republican institutions increasingly claimed authority over the union center. When the largest republic asserted the supremacy of its own laws, it further hollowed out the cohesion of the Soviet state.

questions.prompt: What term describes the rapid, comprehensive market reforms implemented in Russia in January 1992?

choices.text: Shock therapy
Gradualism
choices.note: Gradualism was the alternative approach favored by countries like China and some Russian economists; Russia chose the opposite path.

choices.text: Import substitution
choices.note: Import substitution is a developing-world trade strategy, not a description of Russia's market transition.

choices.text: The Washington Consensus
choices.note: The Washington Consensus was the broader set of free-market policy recommendations from international institutions, not the specific name for Russia's reform approach.

questions.explanation: Shock therapy, implemented under Prime Minister Yegor Gaidar, involved the sudden liberalization of prices, removal of trade barriers, and rapid privatization. While it aimed to quickly create market institutions, it also caused severe inflation, a collapse in living standards, and a dramatic economic contraction in the early 1990s.

questions.prompt: Russia's 'loans-for-shares' scheme of 1995–1996 is most associated with:

choices.text: The transfer of major state enterprises to well-connected bankers at far below market value
A World Bank program providing development loans to Russian small businesses
choices.note: Loans-for-shares was a domestic Russian privatization scheme, not an international development program.

choices.text: A mutual exchange of debt between Russia and former Soviet republics
choices.note: The scheme involved the privatization of Russian state assets, not inter-state debt arrangements.

choices.text: Emergency IMF lending to stabilize the ruble during hyperinflation
choices.note: While the IMF did provide loans to Russia, loans-for-shares was a privatization mechanism that created the oligarchs.

questions.explanation: Loans-for-shares was a controversial privatization scheme in which the Russian government gave shares in major state enterprises (especially in oil and metals) to a handful of well-connected bankers as collateral for loans. When the government predictably defaulted, the bankers kept the shares. This is widely seen as the origin of Russia's oligarch class.

questions.prompt: What major economic event hit Russia in August 1998?

choices.text: A sovereign debt default and sharp devaluation of the ruble
The discovery of massive oil reserves in Siberia that transformed the economy
choices.note: Siberian oil reserves were discovered decades earlier; the 1998 crisis was a financial collapse, not a resource boom.

choices.text: Russia's admission to the World Trade Organization
choices.note: Russia did not join the WTO until 2012.

choices.text: The nationalization of all private banks by the Russian government
choices.note: Banks were not nationalized in 1998, though many went bankrupt during the crisis.

questions.explanation: In August 1998, Russia defaulted on its sovereign debt and devalued the ruble, triggering a severe financial crisis. The crisis wiped out savings, bankrupted banks, and sent shockwaves through global markets. Paradoxically, the devaluation ultimately helped Russian industry by making imports expensive and domestic production competitive, contributing to the economic recovery of the 2000s.

questions.prompt: How did Ukraine's approach to economic reform in the early 1990s differ from Russia's?

choices.text: Ukraine pursued slower, more gradual reforms and delayed large-scale privatization
Ukraine implemented even more radical shock therapy than Russia
choices.note: Ukraine was slower to reform, not faster; it delayed price liberalization and privatization.

choices.text: Ukraine immediately adopted the euro as its currency
choices.note: Ukraine introduced its own currency, the hryvnia, in 1996; it never adopted the euro.

choices.text: Ukraine successfully avoided any economic contraction during the transition
choices.note: Ukraine experienced one of the most severe economic contractions of any post-Soviet state, with GDP falling roughly 60% during the 1990s.

questions.explanation: Ukraine took a more gradual approach to economic reform than Russia, delaying large-scale privatization and maintaining more state control over prices. However, this did not spare Ukraine from economic hardship — it experienced hyperinflation in 1993 and one of the deepest GDP collapses in the post-Soviet space, partly due to delayed reforms and the economic disruption of separating from the Soviet system.

questions.prompt: What role did rising oil prices play in Russia's economic trajectory under Putin in the 2000s?

choices.text: They fueled rapid economic growth that bolstered Putin's popularity and state capacity
They had little effect because Russia had diversified away from resource dependence
choices.note: Russia remained heavily dependent on oil and gas exports; the energy sector accounted for a large share of government revenue.

choices.text: They caused economic stagnation because Russia lacked the infrastructure to export oil
choices.note: Russia had extensive Soviet-era pipeline infrastructure and was one of the world's largest oil exporters.

choices.text: They primarily benefited Ukraine rather than Russia
choices.note: Ukraine is not a major oil producer; it actually suffered from high energy import costs.

questions.explanation: Rising global oil prices in the 2000s were a major driver of Russia's economic boom under Putin. Oil and gas revenues funded increased government spending, rising living standards, and the rebuilding of state capacity. This resource windfall bolstered Putin's popularity but also deepened Russia's dependence on energy exports — a vulnerability exposed when prices dropped.

questions.prompt: What was voucher privatization in early 1990s Russia?

choices.text: A program that gave citizens vouchers that could be exchanged for shares in state enterprises
An International Monetary Fund program that issued food vouchers during hyperinflation
choices.note: Voucher privatization was a domestic scheme for transferring ownership of state property, not an IMF welfare program.

choices.text: A land reform that redistributed collective farms equally to all rural households
choices.note: Voucher privatization was mainly about industrial and commercial enterprises rather than a blanket redistribution of farmland.

choices.text: A debt-relief plan that cancelled Soviet-era household savings obligations
choices.note: The vouchers represented claims on state assets, not debt cancellation for households.

questions.explanation: Voucher privatization was designed to create mass private ownership by giving Russian citizens vouchers that could be invested in formerly state-owned firms. In practice, many citizens sold their vouchers cheaply, while insiders and emerging financiers accumulated large stakes, helping shape the unequal ownership structure of the post-Soviet economy.

questions.prompt: What was the immediate economic effect of Russia's January 1992 price liberalization?

choices.text: Consumer prices surged after state controls were lifted
The ruble became fully stable and convertible overnight
choices.note: Lifting price controls did not instantly stabilize the ruble; the early transition was marked by severe inflation and instability.

choices.text: Inflation disappeared because shortages ended immediately
choices.note: Some shortages eased, but inflation exploded rather than disappearing.

choices.text: Foreign investment flooded in at once and solved the budget crisis
choices.note: Russia did not receive an immediate wave of investment that solved its fiscal problems in early 1992.

questions.explanation: When the Russian government freed most prices in January 1992, years of suppressed inflation and shortages were released into the open. Prices shot upward, savings were devastated, and living standards plunged, even as some goods reappeared on store shelves. This shock became one of the defining social traumas of the early transition.

questions.prompt: Why was the introduction of the hryvnia in 1996 important for Ukraine's transition?

choices.text: It replaced an unstable interim currency and helped anchor macroeconomic stabilization
It permanently pegged Ukraine's economy to the Russian ruble
choices.note: The hryvnia was Ukraine's own national currency, not a mechanism for tying Ukraine back to the ruble zone.

choices.text: It marked Ukraine's entry into the eurozone
choices.note: Ukraine has never joined the eurozone; the hryvnia was a domestic currency reform.

choices.text: It ended the need for any cooperation with the International Monetary Fund
choices.note: Ukraine continued to engage with international financial institutions after introducing the hryvnia.

questions.explanation: Ukraine introduced the hryvnia in 1996 to replace the temporary coupon-karbovanets after years of monetary instability and hyperinflation. The new currency was part of a broader stabilization effort that helped restore some macroeconomic order, even though Ukraine's transition remained difficult and uneven.

questions.prompt: Why was Ukraine hit especially hard by the 2008-2009 global financial crisis?

choices.text: Its economy was heavily dependent on steel exports and external borrowing
It had no banking sector at all
choices.note: Ukraine did have a banking sector, but it was vulnerable to capital flight, currency pressure, and recession.

choices.text: Its oil-export revenues suddenly collapsed
choices.note: Ukraine is not a major oil exporter; its vulnerability was tied more to industrial exports and financing conditions.

choices.text: It had adopted the euro and could not devalue its currency
choices.note: Ukraine was not in the eurozone and retained its own currency.

questions.explanation: Ukraine entered the global financial crisis with major external vulnerabilities, including reliance on steel exports, large financing needs, and a fragile banking system. When global demand collapsed and capital dried up, Ukraine experienced a severe downturn and turned again to the International Monetary Fund for support.

questions.prompt: What was the main purpose of Russia's Stabilization Fund, created in 2004?

choices.text: To save part of the oil-and-gas windfall so the budget would be less vulnerable to future price drops
To finance the immediate privatization of all remaining state enterprises
choices.note: The fund was designed to manage resource windfalls and fiscal volatility, not to underwrite a final wave of privatization.

choices.text: To subsidize the currencies of former Soviet republics
choices.note: Russia's Stabilization Fund was a domestic fiscal instrument, not a regional currency-support mechanism.

choices.text: To replace the Central Bank with a sovereign wealth authority
choices.note: The fund did not replace the Central Bank; it functioned alongside existing monetary institutions.

questions.explanation: As oil prices surged in the 2000s, Russia created the Stabilization Fund to save part of the export windfall rather than spending all of it immediately. The idea was to reduce inflationary pressure, smooth the federal budget across commodity cycles, and build a buffer against future downturns. Later, the fund was split into the Reserve Fund and National Wealth Fund.

questions.prompt: In Russia, why did reformers argue that freeing most prices on January 2, 1992 was necessary?

choices.text: To get food and goods back onto the market by ending shortages caused by fixed prices
To qualify immediately for World Trade Organization membership
choices.note: Russia did not join the WTO until 2012, and price liberalization in 1992 was driven by domestic crisis management.

choices.text: To restore the Soviet central planning system on a more efficient basis
choices.note: Price liberalization was meant to dismantle core features of the command economy, not revive central planning.

choices.text: To introduce a new national currency in place of the ruble
choices.note: Russia kept the ruble; the issue in early 1992 was shortages and distorted prices, not replacing the currency.

questions.explanation: Reformers argued that fixed Soviet-era prices were keeping shelves empty because producers had little reason to sell for rubles that could buy almost nothing. Once prices were liberalized, shortages eased quickly, even though inflation surged.

questions.prompt: Which missing component meant Russia did not carry out full 'shock therapy' in the classic sense in 1992?

choices.text: Sustained macroeconomic stabilization through tight control of money supply and central bank credit
Any serious price liberalization
choices.note: Russia did liberalize most prices very rapidly in January 1992.

choices.text: Any attempt to privatize state enterprises
choices.note: Russia did pursue rapid privatization, especially through the voucher program.

choices.text: Any opening to foreign trade
choices.note: Trade barriers were reduced substantially even if some licenses and quotas remained.

questions.explanation: The key gap was stabilization: reformers did not fully control the central bank or budget politics in 1992, so credit expansion and inflation continued. In the Latin American cases that inspired 'shock therapy,' breaking hyperinflation was the core task.

questions.prompt: What most directly fueled Ukraine's hyperinflation in 1992-1993?

choices.text: Printing money to cover spending and subsidies while stabilization lagged
An immediate switch to a hard-currency peg with the US dollar
choices.note: Ukraine did not solve the problem through a hard peg in those years; the problem was excessive money creation.

choices.text: The rapid elimination of all industrial subsidies
choices.note: Subsidies were not rapidly eliminated; continued subsidies were part of the inflation problem.

choices.text: A sudden collapse in agricultural exports after WTO accession
choices.note: Ukraine was nowhere near WTO accession in 1992-1993, and hyperinflation predated that issue.

questions.explanation: Ukraine combined partial liberalization with weak fiscal and monetary discipline, so the state kept financing deficits by printing money. Inflation was extreme even by post-Soviet standards, and only later stabilization made the hryvnia possible in 1996.

questions.prompt: Why did Russia's voucher privatization often fail to create effective corporate governance?

choices.text: Ownership was dispersed in a weak institutional environment, allowing managers and insiders to strip assets
The state banned private shareholders from voting in company elections
choices.note: The problem was not a formal ban on voting rights but weak enforcement, insider dominance, and poor investor protection.

choices.text: Most large enterprises remained fully under military control
choices.note: Many enterprises were privatized; the issue was how they were governed after privatization.

choices.text: Foreign investors were legally required to manage all privatized firms
choices.note: Foreign management was not the defining feature of voucher privatization in Russia.

questions.explanation: Voucher privatization created ownership fast, but not the institutions needed to discipline managers. With weak courts, regulators, and law enforcement, many firms became targets for insider takeovers and asset stripping rather than productive restructuring.

questions.prompt: What is the best summary of Ukraine's slower privatization path in the 1990s?

choices.text: It delayed sales, but still allowed insider grabs and asset stripping rather than producing a notably fairer system
It prevented the rise of oligarchic wealth by keeping major assets broadly distributed
choices.note: Ukraine still developed powerful oligarchic groups despite moving more slowly than Russia.

choices.text: It produced a highly transparent system dominated by outside investors from the start
choices.note: Privatization was often distorted, delayed, and shaped by insiders rather than clean outsider investment.

choices.text: It left almost all major enterprises fully state-owned through the 2000s
choices.note: Privatization did proceed, though slowly and unevenly, and private-sector output rose over time.

questions.explanation: Ukraine's gradualism did not automatically yield better outcomes. Parliament repeatedly blocked or reshaped privatization, but insiders still captured assets through leasing schemes, political connections, and later cash sales.

questions.prompt: By the late 1990s, what did widespread barter in Ukraine most clearly signal?

choices.text: That much of the economy remained weakly monetized and firms were evading hard budget constraints
That the hryvnia had become one of Europe's strongest currencies
choices.note: Barter spread because monetary relations were weak, not because the currency was unusually strong.

choices.text: That consumer banking had become the main engine of growth
choices.note: Late-1990s Ukraine was marked by weak banking and informal exchange, not consumer-credit-led growth.

choices.text: That privatization had been fully completed and firms no longer needed the state
choices.note: Barter was associated with continuing state weakness, arrears, and semi-reformed economic relations.

questions.explanation: Barter reflected a broader failure of remonetization: firms often settled obligations through non-cash deals, tax arrears, and informal exchange. Wage arrears and nominal employment also masked the depth of the crisis.

questions.prompt: A key aim of Yushchenko's 1999-2001 reforms in Ukraine was to require taxes and many energy payments to be made in cash. What broader problem was this meant to fix?

choices.text: The barter-based, under-monetized economy
Excessive dependence on foreign direct investment
choices.note: The immediate problem was not too much FDI but weak cash settlement, barter, and arrears.

choices.text: The overvaluation of the euro in Ukrainian trade
choices.note: Ukraine did not use the euro domestically, and the reform targeted domestic payment practices.

choices.text: The collapse of agricultural exports after land collectivization
choices.note: The reform focus was on remonetization and fiscal discipline, not a new round of collectivization.

questions.explanation: Cash-only payments forced firms to operate in money rather than opaque barter chains. That helped increase transparency, improve tax collection, and bring more activity back into the formal economy.

questions.prompt: According to the course materials, many of Ukraine's earliest big oligarchic fortunes were rooted in which sector?

choices.text: Gas trade and transit schemes
Consumer electronics exports to Western Europe
choices.note: Ukraine was not known for an early post-Soviet boom in consumer electronics exports.

choices.text: Mass retail banking for households
choices.note: Household consumer banking was underdeveloped; early oligarchic fortunes came from rent-rich sectors.

choices.text: Software outsourcing to US firms
choices.note: Ukraine's tech sector became more important later; it was not the main source of the first oligarchic fortunes.

questions.explanation: Course materials emphasize gas as a key source of early oligarchic wealth in Ukraine, through transit rents, subsidized pricing, nonpayment schemes, and politically connected intermediaries. Steel and other sectors became crucial too, but often later.

questions.prompt: Which comparison between Russia and Ukraine best fits the course's account of oligarchic capitalism in the 1990s?

choices.text: Oligarchs had influence in both countries, but in Ukraine they were more directly embedded in parties, parliament, and executive politics
Russia had no oligarchs in politics, while Ukraine had none in business
choices.note: Both countries had oligarchic business-political linkages; the difference was one of degree and form.

choices.text: Ukraine kept oligarchs out of television ownership, unlike Russia
choices.note: In both countries, major media outlets were heavily shaped by oligarchic ownership or control.

choices.text: Russia and Ukraine had identical patterns of oligarchic power
choices.note: The course stresses that oligarchs' direct political roles were generally more pronounced in Ukraine.

questions.explanation: The course contrasts Russia's more mediated oligarchic influence with Ukraine's more openly political oligarchic factions. In Ukraine, major business figures and their allies were more directly embedded in parties, parliament, and top executive politics.

questions.prompt: What was one major engine of Russia's initial recovery in 1999-2001?

choices.text: Import substitution after the ruble devaluation made domestic production more competitive
A rapid rebound driven mainly by rising oil prices and export revenues
choices.note: Higher oil prices later mattered a great deal, but the course stresses that the initial post-1998 rebound was driven more immediately by devaluation and import substitution.

choices.text: Large-scale foreign investment into newly privatized Russian manufacturing
choices.note: Foreign investment did not drive the first stage of recovery in 1999-2001.

choices.text: A consumer-credit boom centered on mortgage and retail lending
choices.note: Consumer lending expanded later; it was not the main early driver of the rebound after the 1998 crash.

questions.explanation: The 1998 crash was painful, but the cheaper ruble made imports costlier and opened space for domestic producers. Early growth was broader than just oil, reaching industry, agriculture, construction, and services before the oil boom became more central.

questions.prompt: Which reform became a hallmark of Putin's early tax policy?

choices.text: A 13 percent flat personal income tax
The abolition of all corporate profit taxes
choices.note: Russia reduced and restructured taxes, but it did not abolish corporate profit taxation.

choices.text: A return to Soviet-style enterprise remittances instead of taxation
choices.note: The point was to modernize tax collection in a market economy, not restore Soviet fiscal practice.

choices.text: A special tax exemption for all barter transactions
choices.note: Early Putin-era reforms sought greater monetization and compliance, not encouragement of barter.

questions.explanation: The flat tax became famous because it symbolized a broader push to simplify taxation and improve compliance. It was part of a wider effort to rebuild fiscal capacity after the weak tax collection of the 1990s.

questions.prompt: Which of the following was part of Russia's early-2000s banking reform package?

choices.text: Creating deposit insurance and raising minimum capital requirements
Replacing all private banks with a single state bank
choices.note: Russia increased regulation but did not abolish the private banking sector.

choices.text: Forbidding banks from using international accounting standards
choices.note: Reform moved in the opposite direction by encouraging more transparent accounting standards.

choices.text: Banning small-business lending in favor of lending only to state firms
choices.note: The reforms aimed to improve the regulatory environment and expand more normal banking functions, including for smaller businesses.

questions.explanation: These reforms were meant to make banking more reliable and useful for a market economy. In the 1990s many banks had been vehicles for arbitrage, speculation, and insider deals rather than stable financial intermediation.

questions.prompt: In the lecture's discussion of Russia's 'resource curse,' what was identified as the main political-economy danger?

choices.text: Resource rents let the state postpone reforms and rely less on bargaining with society through broad taxation
Oil exports automatically force a country to abandon all manufacturing
choices.note: The lecture did not present deindustrialization as automatic or total.

choices.text: Resource rents made it easier to preserve inefficient firms and patronage networks without deep restructuring
choices.note: That can happen, but the lecture emphasized the broader state-society problem created when rulers can rely on rents instead of broad taxation and bargaining.

choices.text: Resource dependence mainly forced Moscow to decentralize political power toward oil-producing regions
choices.note: The course stresses that resource wealth tended to strengthen central autonomy, not force major political decentralization.

questions.explanation: The lecture stressed that the problem is not only economic volatility. Easy rents can reduce pressure to diversify, reform, and build accountable institutions, because the state can rely heavily on resource income rather than broad taxation of society.

questions.prompt: How did Putin-era political economy differ from the Yeltsin-era model in strategic sectors?

choices.text: The state reasserted oversight, and state-controlled firms expanded their role in oil and heavy industry
The Kremlin withdrew almost completely from the economy after 2000
choices.note: Putin-era Russia is often described as more state-led, not less, in strategic sectors.

choices.text: Gazprom and Rosneft were fully privatized to foreign investors
choices.note: The state retained or increased influence over major strategic firms rather than fully privatizing them away.

choices.text: Heavy industry was broken into small worker cooperatives
choices.note: The trend was toward consolidation and state oversight, not a cooperative model.

questions.explanation: The 2000s saw a shift toward state capitalism in key sectors. The state's share of oil output rose sharply, and large state corporations or state-linked firms became central tools of industrial and political control.

questions.prompt: What was the purpose of creating large Russian state corporations such as the United Aircraft Corporation in the 2000s?

choices.text: To consolidate strategic industries and channel state-led modernization
To reorganize strategic sectors into holding companies that could then be privatized rapidly to outside investors
choices.note: These corporations were associated with consolidation and state guidance, not preparation for rapid sell-offs to outside investors.

choices.text: To separate aviation and shipbuilding from defense priorities and leave modernization mainly to market competition
choices.note: The point was more state coordination in sectors treated as strategic, not less.

choices.text: To consolidate fragmented assets while avoiding ongoing state direction over investment and industrial policy
choices.note: These corporations were created precisely as vehicles for continued state direction over strategic sectors.

questions.explanation: These new corporations reflected a state-led approach to industrial policy. The idea was to merge fragmented assets, invest in modernization, and build national champions in sectors like aviation, shipbuilding, and technology.

questions.prompt: Why was Ukraine's growth boom in the 2000s considered fragile?

choices.text: It relied heavily on high export prices, cheap Russian gas, and easy credit rather than deep institutional upgrading
It depended mainly on a large domestic semiconductor industry
choices.note: Ukraine did not build its boom around a major semiconductor sector.

choices.text: It was driven by joining the eurozone, which later collapsed
choices.note: Ukraine never joined the eurozone.

choices.text: It was based on eliminating all dependence on commodity exports
choices.note: Commodity-linked exports remained central to Ukraine's growth pattern.

questions.explanation: The boom looked impressive, but much of it rested on favorable external conditions. Cheap energy inputs and strong prices for metals, chemicals, and other exports helped growth without necessarily solving underlying institutional weaknesses.

questions.prompt: Why did the post-Orange Revolution reprivatization drive worry many investors in Ukraine?

choices.text: Even limited renationalizations created uncertainty about property rights and looked like oligarchic score-settling
The government announced it would nationalize every large private firm
choices.note: Only a small number of firms were actually renationalized and sold again.

choices.text: Ukraine abolished private ownership of land and housing
choices.note: The reprivatization debate centered on contested large assets, not a blanket abolition of private property.

choices.text: Foreign investors were formally banned from all future tenders
choices.note: The concern was uncertainty and selective intervention, not a universal legal ban.

questions.explanation: Reprivatization was popular with many citizens because it targeted suspect deals, but it also scared business elites and investors. Even a limited campaign could signal that politically connected winners might be reversed by the next coalition.

questions.prompt: Why did Ukraine's later flat-tax move not transform the business climate as much as Russia's did?

choices.text: It was not paired with similarly strong judicial, banking, and administrative reforms
Ukraine set its tax rates only in US dollars
choices.note: Ukraine did not require domestic taxes to be assessed only in dollars.

choices.text: The reform banned firms from using commercial banks
choices.note: Ukraine's problem was not a legal ban on banking but weaker complementary institutions.

choices.text: WTO rules forced Ukraine to cancel tax collection entirely
choices.note: WTO membership did not eliminate the state's power to collect taxes.

questions.explanation: A tax reform can help, but it works better when backed by broader improvements in courts, banking, administration, and enforcement. The course contrasts Russia's more coordinated early-2000s reforms with Ukraine's thinner institutional follow-through.

questions.prompt: Which export pattern made Ukraine resemble a resource-dependent economy in the 2000s?

choices.text: Exports were dominated by metals, minerals, chemicals, and agricultural products, while high-tech exports remained limited
Most exports came from software, pharmaceuticals, and advanced consumer electronics
choices.note: Those sectors did not dominate Ukraine's export profile during the 2000s boom.

choices.text: Ukraine stopped exporting industrial goods and relied almost entirely on tourism
choices.note: Tourism was not the core of Ukraine's export economy.

choices.text: The country shifted overwhelmingly to financial-service exports after WTO entry
choices.note: Financial services were not the defining export success story of 2000s Ukraine.

questions.explanation: Ukraine was not a major oil exporter like Russia, but its export basket was still heavily concentrated in commodity-like sectors. That made growth vulnerable to world prices and left high-tech manufacturing underdeveloped.

questions.prompt: What problem was the Skolkovo project meant to address in Russia?

choices.text: Overdependence on raw-material exports and weak diversification into high-tech sectors
The need to restore collective farms to Soviet levels of output
choices.note: Skolkovo was framed as an innovation and technology initiative, not an agricultural collectivization project.

choices.text: The lack of a national currency after the Soviet collapse
choices.note: Russia had long had the ruble; Skolkovo addressed innovation and diversification, not currency creation.

choices.text: The absence of any domestic oil-refining capacity
choices.note: Russia already had major oil and energy infrastructure; the issue was broader diversification beyond resource dependence.

questions.explanation: Associated especially with the Medvedev period, Skolkovo was supposed to help Russia move into fields like IT, energy technology, and biomedicine. Its mixed record highlights how difficult top-down innovation policy can be in a resource-rich, state-led system.

questions.prompt: The 1994 Budapest Memorandum is significant because it provided Ukraine with:

choices.text: Security assurances from Russia, the US, and the UK in exchange for giving up its nuclear weapons
Membership in NATO's Partnership for Peace program
choices.note: Ukraine did join the Partnership for Peace in 1994, but that was a separate agreement from the Budapest Memorandum.

choices.text: A bilateral defense treaty with the United States
choices.note: The Budapest Memorandum provided assurances, not a binding mutual defense treaty.

choices.text: Financial compensation from Russia for Soviet-era environmental damage
choices.note: The memorandum addressed security guarantees related to nuclear disarmament, not environmental compensation.

questions.explanation: The Budapest Memorandum (1994) was a diplomatic agreement in which Ukraine agreed to transfer its Soviet-era nuclear arsenal (the world's third-largest) to Russia. In return, Russia, the United States, and the United Kingdom provided assurances respecting Ukraine's sovereignty and territorial integrity — assurances Russia violated with its 2014 annexation of Crimea.

questions.prompt: Which countries were included in NATO's first post-Cold War enlargement in 1999?

choices.text: Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic
Poland, Ukraine, and the Baltic states
choices.note: Ukraine has never joined NATO, and the Baltic states joined in 2004, not 1999.

choices.text: Germany, Poland, and Romania
choices.note: Germany (as a unified state) was already in NATO; Romania joined in 2004.

choices.text: Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus
choices.note: None of these former Soviet states have joined NATO.

questions.explanation: NATO's first post-Cold War enlargement in March 1999 admitted Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic — all former Warsaw Pact members. This expansion was highly controversial, with critics like George Kennan warning it would provoke Russia, and supporters arguing it consolidated democracy in Central Europe.

questions.prompt: In his influential 2014 article, political scientist John Mearsheimer argued that the Ukraine crisis was primarily caused by:

choices.text: Western provocation through NATO expansion and EU enlargement toward Russia's borders
Deep ethnic divisions within Ukrainian society
choices.note: Mearsheimer's argument focused on great-power geopolitics and Western strategic overreach, not internal Ukrainian divisions.

choices.text: Russia's inherent imperial ideology dating back to the tsarist era
choices.note: Mearsheimer specifically rejected the view that Russian expansionism was driven by ideology or culture; he emphasized structural geopolitical factors.

choices.text: Economic competition between Russian and European energy companies
choices.note: Mearsheimer's analysis was rooted in realist international relations theory about spheres of influence, not economic competition.

questions.explanation: In 'Why the Ukraine Crisis Is the West's Fault' (Foreign Affairs, 2014), Mearsheimer argued from a realist perspective that NATO and EU expansion into Russia's sphere of influence provoked Moscow's aggressive response. His argument generated major debate, with critics contending it ignored Ukrainian agency and Russian revisionism.
sources: Mearsheimer, 'Why the Ukraine Crisis Is the West's Fault', Foreign Affairs, 2014

questions.prompt: Russia annexed Crimea from Ukraine in 2014 following what sequence of events?

choices.text: The Euromaidan revolution ousted Yanukovych, and Russia sent troops to seize the peninsula
A popular referendum in Crimea preceded any Russian military involvement
choices.note: Russian special forces (the 'little green men') seized key infrastructure before any referendum was held; the referendum occurred under military occupation.

choices.text: NATO forces entered eastern Ukraine, prompting a Russian defensive response
choices.note: NATO did not send forces into Ukraine; Russia's actions were offensive, not defensive.

choices.text: Ukraine voluntarily ceded Crimea as part of a negotiated peace agreement
choices.note: Ukraine has never recognized the annexation of Crimea and considers it illegal occupation.

questions.explanation: After the February 2014 Euromaidan revolution toppled President Yanukovych, Russia rapidly deployed special forces ('little green men') to seize control of Crimea. A hastily organized referendum, conducted under military occupation and without international monitoring, produced a claimed 97% vote to join Russia. The annexation was condemned by most of the international community as a violation of international law.

questions.prompt: What was the main argument made by critics of NATO enlargement in the 1990s, such as diplomat George Kennan?

choices.text: That expanding NATO eastward would be seen as a threat by Russia and poison post-Cold War relations
That NATO was too expensive for new member states to afford
choices.note: While cost was discussed, Kennan's critique was fundamentally about the geopolitical consequences for Russia relations.

choices.text: That the new member states were too democratic and would undermine NATO's military effectiveness
choices.note: If anything, concerns about new members related to whether they were democratic enough, not too democratic.

choices.text: That NATO should have been dissolved after the Cold War ended
choices.note: Kennan did not argue for NATO's dissolution; he specifically opposed its expansion as unnecessarily provocative.

questions.explanation: George Kennan, the architect of Cold War containment policy, called NATO enlargement 'the most fateful error of American policy in the entire post-Cold War era' in 1997. He warned it would inflame Russian nationalism, undermine reformers, and restore Cold War tensions — a prediction that many scholars argue proved prescient.

questions.prompt: At NATO's Bucharest summit in 2008, what position did the alliance take on Ukraine and Georgia?
